title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Nine: In which Michael has trouble with a spell (Fragment 2)
text:
“What’s he doing, do you think?” Calcifer asked Sophie.

Michael gave up blowing and started mashing everything, paper and all, with a pestle and mortar, looking at the skull expectantly from time to time. Nothing happened, so he tried different ingredients from bags and jars.

“I feel bad about spying on Howl,” he announced as he pounded a third set of ingredients to death in a bowl. “He may be fickle to females, but he’s been awfully good to me. He took me in when I was just an unwanted orphan sitting on his doorstep in Porthaven.”

“How did that come about?” asked Sophie as she snipped out another blue triangle.

“My mother died and my father got drowned in a storm,” Michael said. “And nobody wants you when that happens. I had to leave our house because I couldn’t pay rent, and I tried to live in the streets, but people kept turning me off doorsteps and out of boats until the only place I could think of to go was somewhere everyone was too scared of to interfere with. Howl had just started up in a small way as Sorcerer Jenkin then. But everyone said his house had devils in it, so I slept on his doorstep for a couple of nights until Howl opened the door one morning on his way to buy bread and I fell inside. So he said I could wait indoors while he got something to eat. I went in, and there was Calcifer, and I started talking to him because I’d never met a demon before.”

“What did you talk about?” said Sophie, wondering if Calcifer had asked Michael to break his contract too.

“He told me his troubles and dripped on me. Didn’t you?” said Calcifer. “It didn’t seem to occur to him that I might have troubles as well.”

“I don’t think you have. You just grumble a lot,” Michael said. “You were quite nice to me that morning, and I think Howl was impressed. But you know how he is. He didn’t tell me I could stay. He just didn’t tell me to go. So I started being useful wherever I could, like looking after money so that he didn’t spend it all as soon as he’d got it, and so on.”

The spell gave a sort of whuff then and exploded mildly. Michael brushed soot off the skull, sighing, and tried new ingredients. Sophie began making a patchwork of blue triangles round her feet on the floor.

“I did make lots of stupid mistakes when I first started,” Michael went on. “Howl was awfully nice about it. I thought I’d got over that now. And I think I do help with money. Howl buys such expensive clothes. He says no one’s going to employ a wizard who looks as if he can’t make money at the trade.”

“That’s just because he likes clothes,” said Calcifer. His orange eyes watched Sophie at work rather meaningly.

“This suit was spoiled,” Sophie said.

“It isn’t just clothes,” Michael said. “Remember last winter when we were down to your last log and Howl went off and bought the skull and that stupid guitar? I was really annoyed with him. He said they looked good.”

“What did you do about logs?” Sophie asked.

“Howl conjured some from someone who owed him money,” Michael said. “At least, he said they did, and I just hoped he was telling the truth. And we ate seaweed. Howl says it’s good for you.”

“Nice stuff,” murmured Calcifer. “Dry and crackly.”

“I hate it,” said Michael, staring abstractedly at his bowl of pounded stuff. “I don’t know—there should be seven ingredients, unless it’s seven processes, but let’s try it in a pentacle anyway.” He put the bowl on the floor and chalked a sort of five-pointed star round it.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Nine: In which Michael has trouble with a spell

vocabulary.definition: In a way showing that someone is lost in thought and not fully aware of their surroundings.
vocabulary.example: Michael stared abstractedly at his bowl of pounded stuff.

vocabulary.definition: To declare something publicly or formally.
vocabulary.example: Michael announced his feelings as he worked.

vocabulary.definition: Used to emphasize degree; very or extremely.
vocabulary.example: Michael said Howl was awfully nice about his mistakes.

vocabulary.definition: To make something appear or seem to appear by magic or as a product of imagination.
vocabulary.example: Howl conjured up logs to survive winter.

vocabulary.definition: To summon or bring into existence as if by magic.
vocabulary.example: Howl conjured logs to solve a problem.

vocabulary.definition: Making small sharp noises; dry and brittle in texture.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer described the seaweed as dry and crackly.

vocabulary.definition: The area immediately outside a door, especially the step just outside the house entrance.
vocabulary.example: Michael slept on Howl’s doorstep for a couple of nights.

vocabulary.definition: To cause drops of liquid to fall on someone or something; figuratively to share troubles reluctantly.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer said Howl dripped on him his troubles.

vocabulary.definition: With an expression or feeling of eager anticipation.
vocabulary.example: Michael was looking at the skull expectantly from time to time.

vocabulary.definition: Likely to change frequently, especially in regards to affections or loyalties.
vocabulary.example: He may be fickle to females, but he’s been awfully good to me.

vocabulary.definition: To complain in a quiet or ill-tempered way.
vocabulary.example: Calcifer was said to grumble a lot.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling admiration and respect for something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Michael thought Howl was impressed with Calcifer.

vocabulary.definition: The individual foods or substances that are combined to make a particular dish or mixture.
vocabulary.example: He tried different ingredients from bags and jars.

vocabulary.definition: A bowl in which substances are crushed or ground with a pestle.
vocabulary.example: Michael used a pestle and mortar to mash everything.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling upset or hurt by something said or done.
vocabulary.example: Michael felt unwanted and rejected.

vocabulary.definition: A child whose parents are dead.
vocabulary.example: Michael described himself as an unwanted orphan sitting on Howl's doorstep.

vocabulary.definition: A piece or collection of different things put together.
vocabulary.example: Sophie made a patchwork of blue triangles on the floor.

vocabulary.definition: A five-pointed star figure often used as a magical symbol.
vocabulary.example: Michael drew a pentacle on the floor around the bowl.

vocabulary.definition: A tool with a rounded end used for crushing and grinding substances in a mortar.
vocabulary.example: Michael started mashing everything, paper and all, with a pestle and mortar.

vocabulary.definition: To crush or grind something by repeated blows or pressure.
vocabulary.example: Michael pounded ingredients in a bowl.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling fear or anxiety about something or someone.
vocabulary.example: People were too scared of Howl to interfere.

vocabulary.definition: Damaged or ruined, no longer in good condition.
vocabulary.example: Sophie said the suit was spoiled.

vocabulary.definition: To secretly watch or observe someone.
vocabulary.example: Michael felt bad about spying on Howl.

vocabulary.definition: Not desired or welcome.
vocabulary.example: Michael described himself as an unwanted orphan.

vocabulary.definition: A soft explosive noise or puff, especially in magical or smoky contexts.
vocabulary.example: The spell made a whuff noise then exploded.
